questions.question: Explain Pure ALOHA protocol with throughput formula S = G × e^(-2G). What is the maximum throughput and at what value of G does it occur?
keyPoints: Pure ALOHA allows transmission at any time
Throughput S = G × e^(-2G) where G is average frames per frame time
Maximum throughput occurs at G = 0.5
Vulnerable time = 2T (twice the frame transmission time)

questions.question: Compare Pure ALOHA and Slotted ALOHA. Why is Slotted ALOHA more efficient?
keyPoints: Pure ALOHA: vulnerable time = 2T, Slotted ALOHA: vulnerable time = T
Slotted ALOHA requires clock synchronization
Throughput: Pure = G × e^(-2G), Slotted = G × e^(-G)
Maximum throughput: Pure = 18.4%, Slotted = 36.8%
Slotted ALOHA is twice as efficient due to halved vulnerable period

questions.question: Explain the three types of CSMA persistence methods: 1-persistent, non-persistent, and p-persistent. Which one has highest collision probability and why?
keyPoints: 1-persistent: transmits immediately when channel idle - highest collision probability
Non-persistent: waits random time before checking again - lowest collision probability
p-persistent: for slotted channels, transmits with probability p if idle
1-persistent most aggressive - if two stations waiting, both transmit causing collision
Choice depends on network load and propagation delay

questions.question: What is the purpose of minimum frame size in CSMA/CD? Derive the relationship between minimum frame size, propagation delay, and transmission rate.
keyPoints: Minimum frame size ensures collision detected before transmission ends
For Ethernet: Frame ≥ 2 × Propagation Delay × Bandwidth
If frame too small, collision signal may return after station finished transmitting
Standard Ethernet: 512 bits minimum at 10Mbps (51.2μs propagation)
This guarantees detection of collision at farthest point on network

questions.question: Explain CSMA/CA protocol used in WiFi (802.11). How does it avoid collisions? Describe the RTS/CTS mechanism and NAV.
keyPoints: CSMA/CA used in wireless because collision detection not possible
Uses collision avoidance instead of detection
DCF (Distributed Coordination Function) is basic access mechanism
RTS/CTS handshake reserves channel: Request to Send, Clear to Send
NAV (Network Allocation Vector) is virtual carrier sense - timer set from RTS/CTS/DATA/ACK durations
IFS (Inter-Frame Space) provides priority: SIFS < DIFS < EIFS

questions.question: Draw and explain the throughput comparison of ALOHA, Slotted ALOHA, and CSMA protocols. Which protocol has highest efficiency and why?
keyPoints: Pure ALOHA max throughput: 18.4% at G=0.5
Slotted ALOHA max throughput: 36.8% at G=1
1-persistent CSMA: ~53% at optimal G
Non-persistent CSMA: higher efficiency, depends on propagation delay
CSMA with propagation delay approaching zero approaches 100%
CSMA highest because it senses channel before transmitting, reducing collisions

questions.question: Describe CSMA/CD protocol used in Ethernet. Explain the collision detection process, jam signal, and backoff algorithm. Why is this protocol not suitable for wireless networks?
keyPoints: CSMA/CD: Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Detection
Steps: 1) Listen before transmitting (carrier sense), 2) If idle transmit, 3) If collision detected while transmitting, stop and send jam signal
Jam signal ensures all stations know collision occurred
Binary exponential backoff: wait random time (0 to 2^n-1 slot times) after collision
Minimum frame size ensures collision detected before transmission ends (512 bits for 10Mbps Ethernet)

Not suitable for wireless because: 1) Collision detection requires full duplex capability, 2) Hidden node problem in wireless, 3) Signal fading makes collision detection impractical, 4) Wireless uses CSMA/CA instead

questions.question: Explain the hidden node problem in wireless networks and how CSMA/CA with RTS/CTS solves it. Also explain the different IFS used in 802.11 and their purposes.
keyPoints: Hidden node problem: two stations out of range of each other but both in range of access point - both think channel is free and transmit causing collision at AP
RTS (Request to Send) sent by sender, CTS (Clear to Send) replied by receiver
Both RTS and CTS contain duration field - other stations set NAV
Stations hearing either RTS or CTS know channel reserved, avoid transmission
IFS types: SIFS (Short IFS) - for ACK, CTS, Polls; DIFS (DCF IFS) - for normal data; EIFS (Extended IFS) - after error; PIFS (PCF IFS) - for point coordination
Priority order: SIFS < PIFS < DIFS < EIFS

questions.question: Compare Ethernet (802.3) and WiFi (802.11) MAC protocols. Explain why different MAC protocols are used for wired and wireless networks.
keyPoints: Ethernet uses CSMA/CD, WiFi uses CSMA/CA
Ethernet: collision detection (reactive), WiFi: collision avoidance (proactive)
Ethernet: bus topology, half-duplex originally; WiFi: wireless shared medium
Ethernet minimum frame size 64 bytes; WiFi has variable sizes
WiFi has RTS/CTS, NAV, IFS mechanisms; Ethernet does not

Why different: 1) Wireless cannot detect collisions while transmitting, 2) Hidden node problem unique to wireless, 3) Wireless has signal propagation issues, 4) Full duplex in Ethernet makes detection possible, 5) Different physical layer characteristics
